text: Minimally invasive esophagectomy, the Brigham and Women's Hospital experience. We resented the case of a sixty-eight-year-old gentleman with a history of dysphagia and a fifteen-pound weight loss. A barium swallow study demonstrated partial distal obstruction and an endoscopy demonstrated a large distal mass that was friable. Pathology was consistent with a poorly differentiated adenocarcinoma her tuning positive. At a CT scan, the distal esophageal mass at measure is six point five by four point two by four point one centimeters, and the EUS demonstrated T three NX lesion. PET CT scan demonstrated distal esophageal mass with an SUV of thirteen point five, and hence, he was started on a neoadjuvant chemo radiotherapy treatment, which is platinum and toposide, and fifty forty centigrade. He suddenly got a repeat PET CT scan that demonstrated persistent SUV avidity in the distal esophagus, but no distant lesion, and hence we planned for a noninvasive viral Lewis esophagectomy.

The surgeon begins the laparoscopic approach in the abdomen, identifying the greater curvature and preserving a large omental skirt for later use as an omental patch. The lesser sac is accessed laterally, extending towards the short gastric vessels while avoiding the gastro-epiploic artery and gastric wall. Dissection continues towards the lesser curvature, taking down the gastro-hepatic ligament and advancing to the right crus. The left gastric artery is exposed, and omental attachments are removed to isolate the area. A vascular stapler is used to divide the left gastric artery, ensuring lymph node tissue is included. The gastric conduit is formed by dividing the lesser curvature vasculature and using multiple staple fires, maintaining a four-centimeter diameter from the greater curvature wall to the staple line. Retraction is applied to extend the conduit, and the tip is checked for perfusion. The specimen is attached to the gastric conduit, ensuring the staple line orientation remains correct, verified once pulled into the chest. Two sutures secure the orientation, and the conduit is checked before proceeding to the thoracic cavity.

The patient is positioned in the left lateral cubitus position. The surgeon dissects the esophagus from the mediastinum, carefully dividing subclinal nodal tissue to keep it with the specimen. The esophagus is separated from the posterior mediastinum, avoiding injury to the thoracic duct, with branches clipped using harmonic devices. The dissection extends to the thoracic inlet, maintaining a pleural tent above the esophagus vein to cover the anastomosis. The gastric conduit is delivered into the thoracic cavity, ensuring the staple line orientation is correct. The suture is divided to separate the specimen from the conduit. The proximal esophagus is sharply divided with endoshears for resection, ensuring a flush, flat end for anastomosis. The posterior inferior incision is extended, and the specimen is delivered for pathology. Bleeding is controlled with cautery.

The surgeon examines the end of the esophagus and uses a Foley balloon catheter for dilation if necessary. An EEA stapler is placed into the esophageal opening, with two purse-string sutures securing the esophagus around the anvil. The gastric conduit is pulled up, and the proximal staple line is divided to open the conduit. The EEA stapler handle is advanced into the conduit, and the anvil is attached, forming the anastomosis. The NG tube is advanced, and the open end of the conduit is stapled off. The omentum is sutured over the staple line to form a mantle patch, and chest tubes are placed for drainage.

Post-op, patients are started on J-tube feeds on day three. On day five, they undergo a barium swallow study to make sure there's no leak. And they're usually discharged around day seven on tube feed support with an oral liquid diet. On subsequent follow-up, once they've advanced on their normal diet, their tube feeds are discontinued at six weeks. And follow-up CT scans are usually every four to six months based on ultimate pathology. Thank you.
